envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: All Other Miscellaneous Schools and Instruction

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Employer and institutional customers buy scheduled public seats or on-site group classes, commonly priced per participant or per cohort, with repeat demand driven by employee turnover, credential expiration, and regulatory or workplace policy requirements.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 611699 combines materially different models, including yoga schools, public-speaking and speed-reading instruction, survival training, Bible schools, and first-aid/CPR instruction. The screen is narrowed to recurring outsourced health, safety, emergency-response, and compliance instruction because those firms share credential cycles, employer buyers, practical-skill assessment, and cohort delivery; combining them with consumer spiritual, fitness, hobby, and personal-development schools would make the primitives incoherent.
lens.excluded_activities: Consumer yoga and spiritual schools, Bible schools, public-speaking and speed-reading programs, hobby or self-development instruction, military-combat schools, captive internal training departments, software-only course publishers, and firms whose principal activity belongs in a more specific education NAICS code.
lens.included_activities: Private firms repeatedly providing outsourced health, safety, emergency-response, and compliance-oriented instruction to employers and other organizations, including first-aid, CPR/AED, lifeguard, and closely related skills courses when classified in 611699.

caveats: The BLS staffing pattern is for 611600, which includes fine arts, sports, language, tutoring, driving, and other schools beyond both 611699 and the narrowed lens.
O*NET self-enrichment teachers are broader than compliance and emergency-skills instructors.
The supplied compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and may be distorted by the industry's heterogeneous business mix.
a.change_evidence: A 611699 product-line-matched payroll file and time study from CPR/first-aid and safety-training operators, with hours and wages split among instruction, hands-on assessment, curriculum, administration, credentialing, sales, and travel, would materially revise this value.
a.rationale: In the broader 611600 industry, BLS measured educational instruction and library occupations at 53.47% of employment, office and administrative support at 10.03%, and management at 6.76%; self-enrichment teachers alone were 29.26%. O*NET shows instructor work spanning lesson planning, materials, records, testing, progress monitoring, and direct teaching. AI exposure is high for content adaptation, scheduling, records, communications, written assessment, and sales support, but lower for live demonstration, psychomotor coaching, equipment setup, remediation, and observed skills verification. The range is a wage-weighted task judgment for the narrower compliance-training lens.

d5.bridge: BLS projects private Other Schools and Instruction (611600), a broader industry than 611699 and the narrowed health-and-safety segment, to grow from 550,200 jobs in 2024 to 588,500 in 2034, a 7.0% increase. I translate that ten-year employment projection to a five-year service-demand ratio near 1.04, then widen the range for segment mix. AHA's two-year completion-card validity and OSHA's requirement for adequately trained first-aid personnel in covered circumstances support recurring demand but do not quantify its growth. The broad BLS proxy may overstate this segment if consumer instruction grows faster, or understate it if credential requirements, healthcare employment, and employer safety programs expand faster.
caveats: Employment is an input and not a direct measure of constant-quality training quantity.
BLS 611600 includes multiple industries excluded from the narrowed lens.
AHA card validity and OSHA rules establish recurrence for portions of demand but not a national volume growth rate.
d5.change_evidence: Annual U.S. course-completion and renewal counts from AHA, Red Cross, HSI, major state licensing agencies, and multi-site employers, deflated for price and separated by initial versus renewal training, would replace the broad employment proxy.
d5.rationale: The narrowed basket is replenished by expiring credentials, new hires, regulated workplaces, and organizational safety policies. The available broad-industry projection indicates moderate rather than explosive growth, while online delivery changes format more than the underlying need to train and verify workers.

caveats: No current 611699 product-line firm-count table was found that isolates the narrowed lens.
The supplied 70-firm LMM count is itself an EBITDA-band estimate derived from size classes and a sector margin.
Firms may report neighboring NAICS codes, especially professional training, technical schools, fitness instruction, or educational support services.
e.change_evidence: A business-level classification of all 70 supplied LMM candidates using websites, product revenue, customer type, credential affiliations, instructor roster, and owner dependence would directly measure the eligible share.
e.rationale: The official 611699 definition is a residual category whose illustrative activities include public speaking, survival, speed reading, and yoga, while Census classification materials also place first-aid, CPR, lifeguard, and survival-skills training here. Only a minority of the supplied LMM-band firms are therefore likely to match the narrowed recurring employer-oriented health and safety lens. Within that slice, some firms will still fail eligibility because credentials, customer relationships, or delivery depend on the owner.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 611699 ($3,115,300,610) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 611699 ($5,779,764,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1657 (margins.json[61]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Education EBITDA/Sales 16.57%); revenue/employee $111,492 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 611699); class <5: 4,528 firms, avg revenue/firm $260,006 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $43,083 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 982 firms, avg revenue/firm $595,837 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $98,730 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 796 firms, avg revenue/firm $988,791 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $163,843 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 490 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,749,304 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $455,560 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 47 firms, avg revenue/firm $20,504,404 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,397,580 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 23 firms, avg revenue/firm $39,973,957 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,623,685 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 70

caveats: The estimate is most applicable to CPR, first aid, and similar psychomotor credentials, not every health or safety course.
Some cognitive-only compliance modules can already be supplied entirely by software.
Credential bodies could approve automated skills stations more quickly than assumed.
o.change_evidence: Credential-body rules and completion data split among classroom, blended instructor-verified, remote live, and fully automated skills verification, plus employer acceptance and audit outcomes, would quantify the future operator-required share.
o.rationale: Cognitive instruction can be self-served online, but AHA's blended Heartsaver path still requires a verified hands-on skills session with an instructor, and credentials remain traceable to training centers and instructors. The accountable operator therefore remains necessary for practical coaching, equipment, remediation, identity and completion controls, customer records, and issuance or coordination of credentials. Over five years, improved sensor-equipped manikins and remote proctoring may reduce instructor time without eliminating most accountable-center demand.

caveats: One small-provider rate card is not a national pricing sample.
Credential bodies may capture part of the savings through course materials, card, platform, or affiliation fees.
Volume effects are assigned to d5 and o, and implementation difficulty to rho.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level cohort pricing, renewal quotes, discounts, bid outcomes, class utilization, credential fees, and gross margins from several providers before and after documented automation would reveal actual customer pass-through.
q.rationale: Training is usually sold at a quoted class, cohort, or participant price rather than reimbursed at cost. One U.S. on-site provider posts $85 per participant for full CPR/AED and first-aid training, $50 for partial training, and $45 for an e-learning skills assessment, illustrating both fixed pricing and a lower-priced digital pathway. Operators can retain workflow savings between buying cycles, but employer procurement, group discounts, standardized credentials, public price visibility, and online-course competition should pass through a meaningful share over five years.

caveats: Existing e-learning proves digital delivery feasibility, not that generative AI is approved for controlled course content or final assessment.
Affiliation rules and credentialing platforms differ across AHA, Red Cross, HSI, state, and specialty programs.
The estimate excludes pricing, demand, and valuation effects.
rho.change_evidence: Controlled deployments across several accredited training centers measuring labor minutes, credential errors, class fill, cancellations, learner outcomes, remediation, and audit findings before and after AI workflow changes would materially move the range.
rho.rationale: The workflow is already partly digitized: AHA Heartsaver offers a blended path with an online module and a verified in-person skills session. That lowers integration risk for AI-assisted enrollment, reminders, content support, rostering, documentation, test generation, and instructor preparation. Implementation remains bounded by prescribed curricula, credential-provider systems, privacy, quality control, instructor adoption, and the need to preserve reliable hands-on assessment.

caveats: No national transfer-rate series for 611699 or the narrowed segment was located.
Owner age alone does not establish willingness to sell or operational transferability.
Deaths, internal reorganizations, credential-only changes, and asset sales without a continuing operation are excluded.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of AHA, Red Cross, HSI, and state training-center affiliation ownership changes matched to Secretary of State filings, SBA acquisition loans, broker listings, closures, and owner-age interviews would distinguish qualifying transfers from attrition.
s5.rationale: Local training centers and mobile instructor businesses are often closely held, so owner retirement and succession can create control-transfer opportunities. However, instructor-dependent firms can simply close, shrink, or sell equipment and customer lists rather than transfer as going concerns. Census ABS makes owner-age data available at broader industry levels but does not observe 611699 control transfers.

principal_driver: Recurring credential renewal and employer compliance demand support a standardized workflow in which AI can remove cognitive and administrative labor while practical verification persists.
principal_weakness: The official code is highly heterogeneous, so the narrowed segment may contain relatively few of the supplied LMM firms and lacks direct transaction or product-volume data.
